pr:section.pr:title: Agent workflow instructions
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: The spec-first development workflow injected into CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md during adoption.
pr:section.pr:p: This section's llm:node contains the markdown text that gets wrapped in HTML comment markers and appended to the project's agent file. It describes the six-step spec-first workflow and references to clayers commands for validation, coverage, connectivity, and drift.

pr:section.pr:title: Onboard skill
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Comprehensive codebase onboarding skill for Claude Code, with catch-up mode for filling gaps in existing specs.
pr:section.pr:p: This section's llm:node contains the SKILL.md content for the clayers-onboard skill. The skill is a generic orchestrator that discovers lyr:mode[@name="onboard"] elements from the project spec, resolves dependencies via lyr:requires, and executes each mode's guidance body in topological order.

pr:section.pr:title: Review artifacts skill
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Artifact review and coverage improvement skill for Claude Code with drift root-cause analysis.
pr:section.pr:p: This section's llm:node contains the SKILL.md content for the clayers-review-artifacts skill. The skill is a generic orchestrator that queries lyr:mode[@name="review"] and lyr:mode[@name="catch-up"] elements from the project spec and executes their guidance bodies to improve coverage, connectivity, and resolve drift.

pr:section.pr:title: Brainstorm skill
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Structured design brainstorming skill for Claude Code, producing clayers XML spec files instead of markdown design docs.
pr:section.pr:p: This section's llm:node contains the SKILL.md content for the clayers-brainstorm skill. The skill is a generic orchestrator that queries lyr:mode[@name="brainstorm"] elements from the project spec, follows lyr:requires dependencies, and executes each layer's brainstorm guidance body to produce spec XML.

pr:section.pr:title: Write plan skill
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Implementation plan writing skill for Claude Code, producing pln:plan XML in the clayers spec.
pr:section.pr:p: This section's llm:node contains the SKILL.md content for the clayers-write-plan skill. The skill is a generic orchestrator that reads existing spec nodes, queries the plan layer's lyr:mode[@name="brainstorm"] for plan-creation guidance, and follows its body to produce structured pln:plan XML.

pr:section.pr:title: Execute plan skill
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Plan execution skill for Claude Code, implementing plan items with artifact mapping and status tracking.
pr:section.pr:p: This section's llm:node contains the SKILL.md content for the clayers-execute-plan skill. The skill is a generic orchestrator that reads pln:plan items from the spec, queries the artifact layer's lyr:mode[@name="onboard"] for mapping guidance, and executes each item with TDD, artifact mapping, and status tracking.

pr:section.pr:title: Neighbors CLI command
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: `clayers neighbors ID PATH --json` — one-shot neighbor collector across three edge kinds (explicit relations, terminology references, artifact mappings) with a hub pre-filter and cached peeks. Phase-3 helper for the clayers-context skill.
pr:section.pr:p: This section's llm:node contains the operational description of the subcommand. The command walks the assembled combined document once, collects relations (rel:relation[@from|@to=ID]), terminology refs (inbound via ancestor-with-@id, outbound via descendant trm:ref), and artifact mappings (art:mapping[art:spec-ref/@node=ID]), dedupes by neighbor id keeping the highest-priority edge, applies a K=12 hub pre-filter with per-bucket top-2 diversity when triggered, and fetches peeks from llm:node (preferred) or pr:shortdesc / trm:definition / art:note (fallbacks). Returns one JSON bundle per landing so skills replace 6+N separate `clayers query` calls with a single invocation.

pr:section.pr:title: Context skill
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Query-driven context retrieval skill for Claude Code: finds relevant spec nodes via semantic search, filters with an LLM judge, and traverses three edge kinds (relations, terminology refs, artifact mappings) to assemble a focused brief without flooding the main agent's context with raw XML.
pr:section.pr:p: This section's llm:node contains the SKILL.md content for the clayers-context skill. Unlike the other clayers skills, which orchestrate layer modes, this one dispatches subagents at every phase that risks bulk content: Phase 2 precision-filtering, Phase 3 neighborhood traversal, and neighbor peeks. The main agent's context only receives small structured verdicts until the final markdown brief is assembled.

org:reference.org:purpose: Reference text for the development workflow injected into agent files during adoption.
Reference content for the onboarding skill template generated by adopt --skills.
Reference content for the artifact review skill template generated by adopt --skills.
Reference content for the brainstorm skill template generated by adopt --skills.
Reference content for the write-plan skill template generated by adopt --skills.
Reference content for the execute-plan skill template generated by adopt --skills.
Reference content for the context skill template generated by adopt --skills.
Reference material for the `clayers neighbors` CLI subcommand, used by the context skill's Phase 3.

rel:relation.rel:note: The onboarding skill builds on the workflow instructions, providing detailed phase-by-phase guidance for the same process.
The review skill assumes onboarding has been done and focuses on maintaining coverage as code evolves.
The brainstorm skill builds on the spec-first workflow, producing spec XML as its primary output.
The write-plan skill reads spec nodes created by brainstorming to derive implementation plans.
The execute-plan skill reads plan items created by write-plan and implements them sequentially.
Execute-plan uses artifact mapping patterns from the review-artifacts skill during implementation.
The context skill uses clayers query throughout Phases 3 and 4 to traverse edges and fetch node bodies.
Phase 1 of the context skill relies on clayers search for semantic landing-point discovery; without it, vocabulary mismatch between the user's query and the spec's idiom would tank recall.
Phase 3 of the context skill invokes `clayers neighbors` once per kept landing to collect edges + peeks in a single pass, replacing the older 6+N-query fan-out.

spec:clayers.llm:node:
## Clayers Development Workflow
This project uses [clayers](https://github.com/CognitiveLayers/clayers) for structured, layered specifications with machine-verifiable traceability.
**Spec first, code second.** Before implementing, update the spec to describe what you are building.
1. **Update the spec** in `clayers/` — add prose, terminology, relations 2. **Validate**: `clayers validate clayers/PROJECT/` 3. **Implement** the code 4. **Map spec to code** with artifact mappings, fix hashes 5. **Iterate on quality**: - Coverage: `clayers artifact --coverage clayers/PROJECT/` - Connectivity: `clayers connectivity clayers/PROJECT/` - Drift: `clayers artifact --drift clayers/PROJECT/` 6. **Commit** spec + code together
**Plans go in the spec.** Use the `pln:plan` layer to write implementation plans and save them in the knowledge base (`clayers/`). Plans are versioned, queryable, and linked to the concepts they implement.
**Looking for what to do?** Drive spec coverage to 100%. Map every spec node to implementing code. This naturally leads to implementing everything that was specified.
Install: `cargo install clayers`
See [clayers documentation](https://github.com/CognitiveLayers/clayers) for the full layer reference (prose, terminology, organization, relation, decision, source, plan, artifact, llm, revision).

--- name: clayers-onboard description: > Systematically onboard this project to clayers specifications. Discovers layers and their onboarding modes from the spec, executes them in dependency order, and iterates until all quality metrics are clean. Use when: "onboard to clayers", "create specs", "drive coverage to 100%", "catch up", "fill spec gaps". argument-hint: "[--resume | --catch-up]" allowed-tools: - Bash(clayers validate *) - Bash(clayers artifact *) - Bash(clayers connectivity *) - Bash(clayers query *) - Bash(clayers schema *) ---
# Clayers Onboard
Systematically create a complete clayers specification for **{{PROJECT_NAME}}**.
The goal: every meaningful code construct is described in the spec, mapped via artifact traceability, and verified clean by the tooling.
## Prerequisites
1. **clayers is installed**: run `clayers --version` 2. **Project is adopted**: `.clayers/schemas/` exists and `clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/index.xml` exists. If not, run `clayers adopt .`
## Mode Selection
- **Fresh onboarding** (default): start from the discovery phase - **Resume** (`--resume`): check which acceptance criteria already pass and continue from the first incomplete mode - **Catch-up** (`--catch-up`): query `lyr:mode[@name="catch-up"]` instead of onboard modes to fill gaps in an existing spec
## Phase 1: Codebase Discovery
Before executing layer modes, build a mental model:
1. **Scan the project tree**: identify source directories, languages, build system 2. **Catalog modules**: for each directory/package, note its purpose 3. **Extract domain concepts**: identify domain terms from type names, function names, constants, comments (feeds into terminology layer) 4. **Map dependencies**: what depends on what (feeds into relation layer)
This mental model guides the layer mode execution that follows.
## Phase 2: Layer Mode Discovery
Query the project spec for layers with onboard modes:
```bash clayers query '//lyr:layer' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers query '//lyr:mode[@name="onboard"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Each `lyr:layer` describes a layer. Each `lyr:mode[@name="onboard"]` inside declares how to onboard that layer, including: - `lyr:requires`: prerequisite layers - `lyr:condition`: when this mode applies - `lyr:acceptance`: success criteria - `pr:section` body: step-by-step guidance
## Phase 3: Topological Execution
Build a dependency graph from `lyr:requires` elements and execute modes in topological order:
1. **For each mode in order**: - Check `lyr:condition` against the project state. Skip if it doesn't apply. - Read the mode body (the `pr:section` inside contains the guidance). - Follow any `pr:xref ref="tpl-*"` links to templates in `templates.xml` for XML boilerplate. - Execute the guidance: create spec elements as instructed. - Validate: `clayers validate clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/` - Check `lyr:acceptance` criteria. 2. **If acceptance criteria fail**: re-read the mode guidance and fix gaps before moving on.
## Phase 4: Quality Iteration
After all modes execute, run the full quality suite:
```bash clayers artifact --coverage clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers connectivity clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers artifact --drift clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers validate clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Targets: - **Coverage**: zero unmapped nodes - **Connectivity**: zero isolated nodes - **Drift**: zero drifted mappings - **Validation**: no structural errors
For any metric that fails, re-execute the relevant layer's catch-up or review mode. Iterate until all metrics are clean.
## Reading Mode Guidance
To read a specific mode's guidance:
```bash clayers query '//lyr:mode[@id="mode-trm-onboard"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
The mode body contains a `pr:section` with detailed step-by-step guidance, template references, and anti-patterns. Follow it precisely.
## Catch-Up Mode
When invoked with `--catch-up`, query for catch-up modes instead:
```bash clayers query '//lyr:mode[@name="catch-up"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Execute the same phases 2-4, using catch-up modes instead of onboard modes. Catch-up modes typically focus on gap analysis and targeted fixes rather than full layer construction.

--- name: clayers-review-artifacts description: > Review and improve artifact mappings, relations, and coverage. Discovers review and catch-up modes from the spec, executes them to improve quality metrics. Use when: "review artifacts", "fix drift", "improve coverage", "check connectivity". allowed-tools: - Bash(clayers validate *) - Bash(clayers artifact *) - Bash(clayers connectivity *) - Bash(clayers query *) ---
# Clayers Review Artifacts
Review and improve the quality of **{{PROJECT_NAME}}**'s specification.
## Establish Baseline
Before starting, record current metrics:
```bash clayers artifact --coverage clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers artifact --drift clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers connectivity clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers validate clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Record: total nodes, mapped, exempt, unmapped, drifted, isolated.
## Discover Review and Catch-Up Modes
Query the spec for layers with review modes:
```bash clayers query '//lyr:mode[@name="review"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
And for catch-up modes (needed if drift was detected):
```bash clayers query '//lyr:mode[@name="catch-up"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
## Execute Catch-Up Modes First
If the baseline detected drift, execute catch-up modes for the relevant layers. Each catch-up mode body contains detailed guidance for handling its specific drift type (ARTIFACT DRIFTED, SPEC DRIFTED, UNAVAILABLE).
For each drifted layer: 1. Read the catch-up mode guidance (the `pr:section` body) 2. Follow the drift-resolution steps 3. Run `clayers artifact --fix-node-hash` and `--fix-artifact-hash` as directed 4. Verify: `clayers artifact --drift clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/`
## Execute Review Modes
For each layer with a review mode: 1. Read the mode body (`pr:section` with detailed guidance) 2. Follow the review steps (coverage checks, quality audits, etc.) 3. Verify `lyr:acceptance` criteria are met
The artifact layer's review mode covers: spec-to-code coverage, code-to-spec coverage, exemption audit, and mapping quality. The relation layer's review mode covers connectivity.
## Final Verification
Re-run all quality checks and report improvement:
```bash clayers artifact --coverage clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers artifact --drift clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers connectivity clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers validate clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Report deltas: - Coverage: unmapped before vs. after - Drift: drifted before vs. after - Connectivity: isolated before vs. after

--- name: clayers-brainstorm description: > Design features as structured clayers XML specs. Discovers brainstorm modes from the spec and engages relevant layers to produce prose, terminology, relations, and LLM descriptions before code exists. Use when: "design feature", "brainstorm", "create spec for". allowed-tools: - Bash(clayers validate *) - Bash(clayers query *) ---
# Clayers Brainstorm
Design a feature for **{{PROJECT_NAME}}** as a structured specification before writing code.
## Process
1. **Understand the feature**: ask clarifying questions about purpose, constraints, and success criteria before touching the spec 2. **Discover brainstorm modes**: query the spec for layers with brainstorm modes 3. **Select relevant layers**: not all layers apply to every feature. Check each brainstorm mode's `lyr:condition` against the feature 4. **Execute modes in dependency order**: follow `lyr:requires` 5. **For each mode**: read guidance, create spec elements, validate
## Discover Brainstorm Modes
```bash clayers query '//lyr:mode[@name="brainstorm"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Typical layers with brainstorm modes: - Prose: write sections describing the feature - Terminology: define new domain terms - Relation: link to existing spec nodes - Decision: record design choices - Plan: create implementation plan (after design is stable)
## Execute
For each applicable brainstorm mode: 1. Read the mode body (`pr:section` with step-by-step guidance) 2. Follow any `pr:xref` references to templates in `templates.xml` 3. Create spec elements following the guidance 4. Validate: `clayers validate clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/` 5. Check `lyr:acceptance` criteria
## Validate
After each layer mode:
```bash clayers validate clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Before declaring the brainstorm done, run the full quality suite:
```bash clayers artifact --coverage clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers connectivity clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
The feature design is complete when: - All relevant layer brainstorm modes have their acceptance criteria met - New spec nodes are connected to existing ones via relations - The spec validates cleanly

--- name: clayers-write-plan description: > Create implementation plans as pln:plan XML in the spec. Reads existing spec nodes and produces itemized steps with acceptance criteria. Use when: "write plan", "create implementation plan", "plan feature". allowed-tools: - Bash(clayers validate *) - Bash(clayers query *) ---
# Clayers Write Plan
Create an implementation plan for **{{PROJECT_NAME}}** that lives inside the spec as structured XML.
## Read Existing Spec
Query for nodes that need implementation:
```bash clayers query '//pr:section/pr:title' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ --text clayers query '//trm:term/trm:name' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ --text clayers query '//dec:decision' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Understand what concepts exist, which have implementations (artifact mappings), and which are pending design decisions.
## Read Plan Layer Guidance
```bash clayers query '//lyr:mode[@id="mode-pln-brainstorm"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
The plan layer's brainstorm mode contains detailed guidance for plan structure, item decomposition, and acceptance criteria.
## Create Plan
Follow the plan layer mode guidance. Use the template referenced there:
```bash clayers query '//cnt:content[@id="tpl-plan"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
A plan has: - Title and overview - Status (proposed -> active -> completed) - Items with titles, descriptions, acceptance criteria, and status - Optional links to spec nodes the items implement - Optional witness references for testable criteria
## Validate
```bash clayers validate clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```

--- name: clayers-execute-plan description: > Execute plan items from the spec with TDD, artifact mapping, and status tracking. Use when: "execute plan", "implement plan items", "start implementing". allowed-tools: - Bash(clayers validate *) - Bash(clayers artifact *) - Bash(clayers connectivity *) - Bash(clayers query *) ---
# Clayers Execute Plan
Execute a plan from **{{PROJECT_NAME}}**'s specification.
## Find Plans
```bash clayers query '//pln:plan/pln:title' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ --text clayers query '//pln:plan[pln:status="active" or pln:status="proposed"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Pick an active or proposed plan to execute.
## Read Artifact Layer Guidance
The artifact layer's onboard mode contains the mapping guidance you'll need for each implemented item:
```bash clayers query '//lyr:mode[@id="mode-art-onboard"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
## Execute Items
For each pending item in the plan:
1. **Read the item**: title, description, acceptance criteria 2. **Implement with TDD**: - Write a failing test - Run it, verify it fails - Write minimal implementation - Run it, verify it passes 3. **Create artifact mapping**: follow the artifact layer's onboard mode guidance to link new code to the spec node it implements 4. **Fix hashes**: ```bash clayers artifact --fix-node-hash clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers artifact --fix-artifact-hash clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ``` 5. **Update item status**: change `pln:item-status` from `pending` to `completed` 6. **Validate**: `clayers validate clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/` 7. **Commit**: spec + code together, one commit per item
## After All Items
Run quality checks to verify clean state:
```bash clayers artifact --coverage clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers artifact --drift clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers connectivity clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
Update the plan's status from `active` to `completed`.

--- name: clayers-context description: > Build a focused context brief for a natural-language question against a clayers spec. Runs semantic search to find landing points, filters them with an LLM judge, traverses relation/terminology/artifact edges via subagents, and assembles a tight markdown summary. Keeps the main agent's context lean by fanning heavy reading out to subagents. Use when: "give me context on X", "what does the spec say about X", "how does X work here", "orient me on X in this clayers project". allowed-tools: - Bash(clayers search *) - Bash(clayers query *) - Agent ---
# Clayers Context
Given a user question about **{{PROJECT_NAME}}**, produce a focused markdown brief of relevant spec nodes — terms, prose sections, related nodes, and artifact pointers — without pulling walls of XML into the main agent's context.
This skill fans out work to subagents at every phase that could leak bulk content. The main agent's context only receives small structured verdicts. The full assembled brief is the only large output, and it contains only filtered, relevant material.
## Four phases
### Phase 1 — Landing points (semantic search, multi-variant)
Generate 1–2 paraphrases of the user's question first (cheap LLM work you do directly as the main agent). Example: "staleness detection" → "hash-based drift detection", "spec content change detection". Single query recall is borderline on vocabulary-mismatch questions, so issue multiple variants and union.
Run one CLI call with all variants via the repeatable `--also` flag:
```bash clayers search "<raw>" clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ --also "<paraphrase 1>" --also "<paraphrase 2>" --k 10 --json ```
Output is a JSON array of `{id, file, line_start, line_end, layer, score, text_score, struct_score, preview}` — already unioned by id with max score per node and sorted descending. No manual merge needed. See the `search-multi-query` section in the spec for the semantics.
**Do not apply an absolute score threshold for no-match.** Nonsense queries still produce scores around 0.4. The no-match signal comes from Phase 2 returning empty `keep_ids`.
Optional post-filters you may add to the CLI call when the question shape warrants it:
- `--layer prose --layer terminology` when the question asks about concepts rather than code. - `--xpath '//*[not(ancestor-or-self::art:mapping)]'` to exclude artifact mappings when the user wants conceptual material.
### Phase 2 — Judge (precision filter, one subagent)
Dispatch one subagent with the original user question and all ≤10 landing records (id + preview + score) using this strict contract:
> Return ONLY a JSON object with keys `keep_ids` (list of strings), > `drop_ids` (list of strings), `reasons` (list of strings, one per > id in `keep_ids` then one per id in `drop_ids`, in that order). > Every input id must appear in exactly one of the two lists. Give > short original justifications; do not quote previews back. Return > no prose, no markdown fences, no content outside the JSON.
**If `keep_ids` is empty, stop.** Emit a user-facing message: "No relevant spec material found in clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ for this question." Do not enter Phase 3 or 4. Empty `keep_ids` is the sole no-match signal.
### Phase 3 — Traverse three edge kinds (main agent fans out; subagent judges)
**Why this phase is split main-vs-sub this way.** Claude Opus 4.7 does not support prefill, which means tool-using subagents narrate their reasoning between tool calls no matter how strictly the prompt forbids it. Empirically, subagents that receive pre-fetched data and make only judgment calls comply with strict JSON contracts perfectly (no leakage). So Phase 3 keeps the six structural queries and the peek fetches in the main agent — they are deterministic, small, and do not need LLM judgment — and uses one subagent per landing node for the single thing an LLM must do: decide relevance.
**For each id in `keep_ids`, the main agent runs one `clayers neighbors` call and dispatches one judge subagent with the result.** The CLI does edge discovery, dedupe, hub pre-filter, and peek lookup in a single combined-document pass — replacing the older 6+N `clayers query` fan-out per landing.
```bash clayers neighbors <LANDING_ID> clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ --json ```
Flags (all optional):
- `--hub-threshold N` (default 12) — apply the bucket-diversity hub pre-filter when combined degree exceeds this. - `--top-per-bucket N` (default 2) — when the hub filter engages, keep this many per edge-kind bucket (`artifact-map`, `relation`, `term-ref`). - `--peek-chars N` (default 350) — truncate peek strings.
Output shape:
```json { "landing_id": "...", "degree_observed": 17, "neighbors_by_edge_kind": {"relation": 8, "term-ref": 3, "artifact-map": 6}, "hub_engaged": true, "candidates": [ {"id": "...", "edge_kind": "artifact-map", "peek": "..."}, {"id": "...", "edge_kind": "relation", "edge_subtype": "depends-on", "peek": "..."}, {"id": "...", "edge_kind": "term-ref", "peek": "..."} ] } ```
Then dispatch ONE subagent per landing with the original user question, the bundle, and this contract:
> Return ONLY a JSON object with keys: > > - `node_id` (string, equals the landing id) > - `kept_neighbors` (list of `{id, edge_kind, reason, snippet}`; > `snippet` is one sentence) > - `dropped_count` (integer) > - `degree_observed` (integer; equals the number of candidates you > were given) > - `neighbors_by_edge_kind` (object mapping `edge_kind` → total > candidate count for that kind) > > Every candidate id must appear either in `kept_neighbors` or be > counted in `dropped_count`. `edge_kind` values: `"relation"`, > `"term-ref"`, `"artifact-map"`. > > Response rules: your entire output must be one parseable JSON > object. No markdown fences, no prose preamble or trailing > commentary. Do not quote the peek text back; write fresh one-line > reasons.
This subagent has no tools — it only classifies pre-provided data. That is why its output is reliably narrative-free.
**Traversal control** lives inside `clayers neighbors`:
- **Hub pre-filter at K = 12 with edge-kind diversity** (default). When combined degree > 12, candidates are partitioned into three buckets (`artifact-map`, `relation:*`, `term-ref`) and the top 2 from each bucket are kept, ranked within bucket by relation-type priority (`implements` > `depends-on` > `refines` > `precedes` > `constrains` > rest). Result: 6 neighbors spanning all reachable edge kinds — rare-but-valuable term-refs aren't starved by dense artifact-map traffic. - **Max depth: 1 hop** (from the landing). Phase 2's `keep_ids` already represent the semantically-confirmed first layer; Phase 3 expands each by one step and relies on Phase 4 synthesis to tie them into the answer. - **No peek budget** needed — `clayers neighbors` caps output at `top_per_bucket * 3` = 6 per landing, so peek volume is bounded by keep-set size × 6.
### Phase 4 — Direct answer (main agent)
For each id in the union of `keep_ids` (Phase 2) and kept neighbors (Phase 3), run:
```bash clayers query '//*[@id="ID"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ clayers query '//art:mapping[art:spec-ref/@node="ID"]' clayers/{{PROJECT_NAME}}/ ```
These return full XML. Artifact mappings yield file paths with line ranges — **use those line ranges inline in the answer**. Specific locators like `crates/foo/src/bar.rs:94-175` are high-value for the reader and the single easiest way to let them drill deeper.
**Answer the user's question directly**, synthesizing across the kept nodes. Use structure (**bold labels**, numbered steps, inline code) when it clarifies the answer. Organize by question topic, not by spec id — a document dump with one section per kept node is the failure mode.
### Output template
Typical shape:
``` <Opening sentence or paragraph that directly answers the question, citing specific ids inline in backticks and line ranges where available, e.g.: "Drift is divergence between a spec node and its mapped artifact (`term-drift`), detected via stored C14N hashes against current content (`drift-detection-tooling`, implemented in `crates/clayers-spec/src/drift.rs:94-175`)."
Follow with additional structure as the question warrants: - **Bold labels** with short explanations for list-of-parts answers - Numbered steps for how-does-X-work answers - Inline code for identifiers, file paths, and line ranges
Keep it grounded in the kept nodes. Cite the id (backticks) and path:line-range (where artifact-mapped) each time you make a load-bearing claim.>
References: <id-1>, <id-2>, <id-3>, … <id-N> ```
Rules:
- **No preamble.** Do not say "Now I have the material" or "Let me assemble the brief" or anything before the answer. First output character is the first character of the answer. - **No trailing summary.** The structured answer IS the summary — don't append another "In summary, …" paragraph. - **Don't dump per kept node.** If the same id is relevant to two parts of the answer, cite it in both; it's fine — what you're avoiding is a `## <id>` section per kept node. - **Cite line ranges where you have them.** Phase 3's `artifact-map` candidates carry file paths and line ranges; surface them inline. A claim about code without a locator is weaker than one with `path:start-line-end-line`. - **References line** lists all cited ids, flat, descending relevance. Short — don't re-describe each id on that line. - **Target: ≤ 1200 output tokens.** Concision still wins; structure is for clarity, not volume. Longer is only justified when the question genuinely requires enumeration (listing all N layers, stepping through an N-stage pipeline). - **If Phase 2 returned empty `keep_ids`**, emit the one-line no-match message and stop.
## Subagent return contracts (strict)
- **Phase 2 judge**: `{keep_ids[], drop_ids[], reasons[]}`. Empty `keep_ids` is the no-match signal. - **Phase 3 traverser**: `{node_id, kept_neighbors: [{id, edge_kind, reason, snippet}], dropped_count, degree_observed, neighbors_by_edge_kind}`. `edge_kind` is one of `"relation"`, `"term-ref"`, or `"artifact-map"`.
Never accept subagent output that contains raw XML or narrative prose outside the contract keys. If a subagent leaks, redispatch once with a tightened prompt; on a second leak, drop the leaked content and continue with what parses cleanly.
## When to invoke
Good fits:
- "How does X work in this project?" — pulls concept + implementing code. - "What's the design rationale for Y?" — pulls decisions and related relations. - "Orient me on subsystem Z." — pulls the connected subgraph.
Bad fits (use other skills instead):
- "Onboard me to clayers" — use `clayers-onboard`. - "Brainstorm a feature" — use `clayers-brainstorm`. - "Review artifact coverage" — use `clayers-review-artifacts`.

art:mapping.art:note: plant_skills extracts and writes the review-artifacts skill from embedded guidance XML to .claude/skills/.
plant_skills extracts and writes the onboard skill from embedded guidance XML to .claude/skills/.
extract_from_guidance retrieves workflow content (16-31), amend_agent_file injects it into AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md (378-422).
